network.overall_strategy: This network integrates four literature-validated prebiotic routes to valine: (1) Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic photochemistry via glyceraldehyde → acetone → valinonitrile, (2) classical Strecker synthesis from isobutyraldehyde, (3) Kaur et al. (2024) Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of α-ketoisovalerate, and (4) Miller-Urey spark discharge. The network traces carbon from CO₂ (hydrothermal) and formaldehyde (surface) through formose chemistry to glyceraldehyde, then through two main branches: the cyanosulfidic photoreduction branch yielding acetone/cyanohydrin intermediates, and a cyanohydrin-hydrolysis branch generating α-hydroxyisovaleric acid → α-ketoisovalerate. Five hub molecules (HCN, NH₃, acetone, isobutyraldehyde, valinonitrile) create network density. The key open challenge—prebiotic supply of the branched C₄ skeleton—is addressed via Patel et al.'s demonstrated acetone-to-isobutyraldehyde cyanosulfidic reduction, and α-ketoisovalerate is generated from isobutyraldehyde through a chemically sound cyanohydrin→hydroxy acid→keto acid sequence.

reactions.name: CO2 hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, H2 from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is reduced to formate via surface-bound hydride transfer on greigite (Fe3S4), an analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters. H2 dissociatively adsorbs on the Fe-S surface to generate metal-hydride species, which transfer to CO2.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate is a well-established hydrothermal reaction at alkaline vents. This reaction provides the initial C1 building block for downstream formaldehyde and formose chemistry. Supported by iron-sulfur world hypothesis (Wächtershäuser, 1988) and demonstrated experimentally for CO2→formate conversion on Fe3S4.

reactions.name: Formate photoreduction to formaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (254 nm), shallow pool receiving hydrothermal outflow

reactions.mechanism: UV photons excite TiO2 semiconductor surfaces generating electron-hole pairs. Photoelectrons reduce formate (HCOO⁻) to formaldehyde (HCHO) + OH⁻. Siderite may also participate as UV-active mineral for formate photoreduction.
reactions.reasoning: Formate-to-formaldehyde conversion is thermodynamically uphill but photochemically feasible on TiO2 or siderite under UV. This connects hydrothermal C1 products to surface formose chemistry. TiO2 photoreduction of carboxylates is documented in prebiotic contexts.

reactions.name: Formose initiation: formaldehyde self-condensation to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline evaporitic pool, pH 10-12, formose reaction initiation

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo umpolung-type self-condensation via the Breslow cycle. Under alkaline conditions with Ca(OH)₂, an enolizable formaldehyde tautomer attacks a second HCHO to form glycolaldehyde. Borate minerals stabilize sugar products and suppress the Cannizzaro reaction.
reactions.reasoning: Formose reaction is a well-established prebiotic pathway from HCHO to sugars. Borate stabilization of sugars is documented by Ricardo et al. (2004). Glycolaldehyde is the initial C2 product and feeds into glyceraldehyde synthesis.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation: glycolaldehyde + formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline pool, borate complexation directs aldol selectivity

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde enolizes under alkaline conditions and attacks formaldehyde electrophilically to give glyceraldehyde (C3). Borate minerals complex with vicinal diols of glyceraldehyde, stabilizing the product against degradation.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde with formaldehyde is a standard formose pathway step. Borate stabilization of glyceraldehyde is critical to prevent degradation (Ricardo et al., 2004). Glyceraldehyde is a key precursor for the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic pathway.

reactions.name: Glyceraldehyde to dihydroxyacetone isomerization

conditions.other: keto-enol tautomerization, equilibrium favors DHA slightly

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes base-catalyzed 1,2-enolization to form the enediol intermediate, which re-protonates at C1 to give dihydroxyacetone (DHA). This is a standard Lobry de Bruyn-van Ekenstein isomerization.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde-DHA interconversion is thermodynamically facile and well-documented. In the Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic scheme, glyceraldehyde is converted to DHA as a precursor to acetone. Clay surfaces may catalyze the isomerization.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoreduction of DHA to acetone

conditions.other: UV irradiation (254 nm), H2S as terminal reductant, phosphate buffer

reactions.mechanism: UV light drives Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycling on copper sulfide surfaces. Cu(I) donates an electron to DHA, reducing the terminal hydroxyls to hydrogen, while H2S provides reducing equivalents (being oxidized to elemental sulfur). Net transformation: HOCH2COCH2OH → CH3COCH3 + H2O. This is a photoreductive deoxygenation.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that glyceraldehyde/DHA are photoreduced to acetone under UV in the presence of H2S and Cu(I)/Cu(II) catalysis. This is a critical step in the cyanosulfidic pathway, providing the branched C3 ketone needed for valine's isopropyl skeleton. Cu2S minerals serve as the photocatalytic surface (Patel et al., 2015, Nature Chemistry).

reactions.name: Acetone cyanohydrin formation (compound 23)

conditions.other: dark reaction, mildly basic pH, HCN/CN⁻ addition to ketone

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide anion (CN⁻) acts as a nucleophile attacking the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of acetone. Protonation of the resulting alkoxide gives acetone cyanohydrin ((CH3)2C(OH)CN, compound 23 in Patel et al.). This is a standard cyanohydrin formation.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) described the addition of HCN to acetone as the first step in the valine homologation sequence (Fig. 1b). This dark reaction proceeds readily in aqueous solution at near-neutral pH where CN⁻ is available. The cyanohydrin 23 is an established intermediate en route to valine.

reactions.name: Thioamide formation: cyanohydrin 23 to alpha-hydroxythioamide 24

conditions.other: dark reaction, HS⁻ attacks nitrile carbon, Cu sulfide provides sulfide

reactions.mechanism: Hydrosulfide (HS⁻) performs nucleophilic addition to the nitrile (C≡N) of acetone cyanohydrin 23, generating a thioamide functional group. The product is the α-hydroxythioamide 24, retaining the hydroxyl from the cyanohydrin. This sulfide-mediated nitrile hydration is analogous to nitrile hydratase chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated this dark-phase conversion of cyanohydrin 23 to α-hydroxythioamide 24 using H2S. Cu sulfide minerals can supply HS⁻ from dissolved copper sulfide or from HCN reduction (Patel et al., 2015). This step activates the nitrile for subsequent photoreduction.

reactions.name: Photoreductive homologation: compound 24 to cyanohydrin 26 via compound 25

conditions.other: UV irradiation (254 nm), sequential: (i) photoreduction of 24 with H2S to thioamide 25, losing the α-OH as H2O; (ii) further photoreduction of 25 to an aldehyde (isobutyraldehyde); (iii) immediate HCN trapping of the aldehyde to cyanohydrin 26

reactions.mechanism: This is a multi-step photochemical sequence within the same UV-irradiated environment. First, UV/H2S photoreduces α-hydroxythioamide 24 to thioamide 25 (removing the OH, replacing with H). Then UV/H2S converts thioamide 25 via desulfurization to the corresponding aldehyde (isobutyraldehyde). HCN present in solution immediately traps this transient aldehyde as cyanohydrin 26 (2-hydroxy-3-methylbutanenitrile). This extends the carbon chain from C4 (acetone + CN) to effectively a C5 skeleton (C4 aldehyde + CN).
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry) demonstrated this photoreductive homologation cascade (compounds 24→25→26) as the key chain-extension step in valine synthesis. UV irradiation with H2S drives sequential reductions on Cu sulfide surfaces. The overall ~42% yield from 26 to 27 (next step) was reported, with intermediate yields of 40-70% per step (Table 1, Patel et al., 2015). This is the most experimentally validated prebiotic route to valine's branched skeleton.

reactions.name: Ammonolysis: cyanohydrin 26 to valinonitrile 27

conditions.other: NH3/NH4+ at pH 8-9, displacement of OH by NH2

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia displaces the hydroxyl group of cyanohydrin 26 via an SN2-like or elimination-addition mechanism. The α-hydroxy nitrile is converted to an α-amino nitrile (valinonitrile, compound 27). This is effectively the Strecker aminonitrile formation step, but operating on a pre-formed cyanohydrin rather than starting from aldehyde + NH3 + HCN simultaneously.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated this conversion with ~42% yield of compound 27 from compound 26 (Table 1). This ammonolysis is the penultimate step before hydrolysis to valine. The reaction requires sufficient NH3 concentration at mildly basic pH, consistent with volcanic/geothermal pool settings. Montmorillonite may concentrate reactants at the surface.

reactions.name: Reversible cyanohydrin dissociation: compound 26 to isobutyraldehyde + HCN

conditions.other: reversible under slightly basic pH; dissociation favored at higher pH where HCN is deprotonated

reactions.mechanism: Cyanohydrin 26 undergoes base-catalyzed retro-cyanohydrin reaction: the C-CN bond cleaves heterolytically, releasing CN⁻ and generating isobutyraldehyde ((CH3)2CHCHO). This equilibrium is pH-dependent—at higher pH, CN⁻ deprotonation drives dissociation. The released isobutyraldehyde is a free C4 branched aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanohydrin formation is well-known to be reversible. Compound 26 in Patel et al. (2015) is the cyanohydrin of isobutyraldehyde. Under basic conditions (e.g., pH > 9), the equilibrium shifts toward free aldehyde + CN⁻. This reaction connects the cyanosulfidic pathway to the Strecker pathway by liberating free isobutyraldehyde, which Miller & Schlesinger (1993) identified as 'the Strecker precursor to valine.' This provides a prebiotically grounded source for isobutyraldehyde.

reactions.name: Classical Strecker synthesis: isobutyraldehyde + NH3 + HCN to valinonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Zn2+ ions (stabilize imine), clay surfaces concentrate reactants
conditions.other: pH 8-10, NH3 must be deprotonated (pKaH ~9.2), evaporitic pool or drying pond

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: NH3 attacks the aldehyde carbonyl of isobutyraldehyde, forming a carbinolamine, which dehydrates to an imine (Schiff base, (CH3)2CH-CH=NH). Step 2: CN⁻ attacks the electrophilic imine carbon, forming valinonitrile (2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile, compound 27). Zn2+ can stabilize the imine intermediate, accelerating CN⁻ addition.
reactions.reasoning: Miller & Schlesinger (1993) explicitly identified isobutyraldehyde as 'the Strecker precursor to valine' and noted metal salt catalysis (Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Zn2+) for analogous α-keto acid reactions. Classical Strecker synthesis is one of the oldest known routes to amino acids from aldehydes. Montmorillonite concentrates and aligns reactants in drying-pool settings. ZnS (sphalerite) provides Zn2+ for imine stabilization.

reactions.name: DAP-mediated Strecker synthesis of valinonitrile

conditions.other: neutral pH (~7), DAP enables Strecker at lower pH by forming N-phosphoroaminonitrile intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Diamidophosphate (DAP) reacts with the aldehyde and HCN, forming an N-phosphoroaminonitrile intermediate. This activates NH3 transfer and CN⁻ addition at neutral pH, bypassing the requirement for free NH3 (which is mostly protonated at pH 7). The N-phosphoroaminonitrile then hydrolyzes to release valinonitrile and phosphate.
reactions.reasoning: Powner et al. (2019, Communications Chemistry) showed that DAP promotes Strecker synthesis at neutral pH with 73-quantitative% yield of N-phosphoroaminonitriles from simple aldehydes + HCN. By analogy, DAP-mediated Strecker from isobutyraldehyde should yield valinonitrile under the same mild conditions. DAP itself can form from wet-dry cycling of meteorite-derived phosphides (schreibersite). Phosphate minerals provide DAP precursor.

reactions.name: Valinonitrile hydrolysis to valine

conditions.catalyst: acid or base catalysis; mineral surfaces optional
conditions.other: mild heating in acidic or basic aqueous solution; wet-dry cycling may assist

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (C≡N) of valinonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first, water attacks the nitrile carbon to form an amide intermediate (valinamide), which then hydrolyzes further to valine + NH3 (or valine under acidic conditions). The overall transformation: (CH3)2CHCH(NH2)CN + 2H2O → (CH3)2CHCH(NH2)COOH + NH3.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis is the terminal step in all Strecker-based valine syntheses. Patel et al. (2015) and Miller & Schlesinger (1993) both invoke this step. Nitrile hydrolysis is thermodynamically favorable and proceeds spontaneously in warm aqueous solutions, especially under acidic or basic conditions. Geothermal heating (50-100°C) would accelerate this reaction in prebiotic pools.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin hydrolysis of isobutyraldehyde cyanohydrin to alpha-hydroxyisovaleric acid

conditions.other: isobutyraldehyde + HCN form cyanohydrin in situ, which hydrolyzes to α-hydroxy acid; two-step one-pot process

reactions.mechanism: Isobutyraldehyde reacts with HCN to form cyanohydrin (identical to compound 26 from Patel pathway). Under acidic or basic aqueous conditions, the nitrile group of this cyanohydrin hydrolyzes (via amide intermediate) to the carboxylic acid, yielding 2-hydroxy-3-methylbutanoic acid (α-hydroxyisovaleric acid). The α-OH is retained (unlike the Strecker route where NH3 replaces OH).
reactions.reasoning: Cyanohydrin formation + hydrolysis is the classical route to α-hydroxy acids, directly paralleling the Strecker route but without NH3. Kaur et al. (2024) noted that α-hydroxy acids are co-products of reductive amination alongside amino acids, confirming this competing pathway. This provides a prebiotic route to α-hydroxyisovaleric acid, the immediate precursor to α-ketoisovalerate via oxidation.

reactions.name: Oxidation of alpha-hydroxyisovaleric acid to alpha-ketoisovalerate

conditions.catalyst: UV photocatalysis on TiO2; Fe3+ ions from pyrite weathering
conditions.other: UV irradiation, mild oxidation; Fe3+ acts as one-electron oxidant

reactions.mechanism: The α-hydroxyl group of α-hydroxyisovaleric acid is oxidized to a ketone, yielding α-ketoisovalerate (3-methyl-2-oxobutanoic acid). This can proceed via UV-TiO2 photocatalytic oxidation (generating hydroxyl radicals that selectively abstract the α-H) or by Fe3+-mediated electron transfer (Fe3+ → Fe2+ + radical, followed by radical recombination to give the ketone).
reactions.reasoning: α-Hydroxy acid to α-keto acid oxidation is thermodynamically favorable and can be driven by UV photochemistry or Fe3+ oxidation. This provides a prebiotically plausible route to α-ketoisovalerate, which Kaur et al. (2024) used as the starting material for reductive amination. The prebiotic source of α-ketoisovalerate is identified as a key open question in the literature; this cyanohydrin hydrolysis → oxidation route addresses that gap using isobutyraldehyde from the cyanosulfidic pathway.

reactions.name: Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.catalyst: Ni nanoparticles or Ni-Fe sulfide minerals (pentlandite)
conditions.other: pH 7-8, NH4HCO3 buffer (150 mM), 72h reaction time; Ni-bearing meteorite powder also effective

reactions.mechanism: H2 dissociatively adsorbs on Ni-containing mineral surfaces, generating metal-hydride species. The α-keto acid (α-ketoisovalerate) first forms an imine with NH3 (via condensation of the ketone with ammonia), then the metal-hydride reduces the C=N imine bond to give the amino acid (valine). Competing α-hydroxy acid formation occurs via direct C=O reduction without NH3.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. (2024, Chem) demonstrated this reaction explicitly: Ni nanoparticles or FeNi meteorite powder catalyzed H2-driven conversion of α-ketoisovalerate to valine at 22°C, pH 8, 5 bar H2. Combined amino + hydroxy acid yields were 6-84% depending on catalyst (Ni gave ~50%). Pd gave 80-90% yield with >5:1 amino:hydroxy selectivity. No reaction occurred without metal catalyst. Natural analogs include pentlandite and awaruite (Ni3Fe) in alkaline hydrothermal vents.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on Ni catalyst

conditions.other: pH 7-8, NH4HCO3, analogous conditions to rxn_017

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate condenses with NH3 to form an imine, which is reduced by H2 on Ni surface to give alanine. Identical mechanism to rxn_017 but with the C3 substrate pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. (2024) demonstrated reductive amination of multiple α-keto acids including pyruvate to the corresponding amino acids. Alanine production from pyruvate is well-established in this system. Alanine serves as an amino group donor for transamination reactions downstream.

reactions.name: Metal-catalyzed transamination: alpha-ketoisovalerate + alanine to valine + pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+/Cu2+ ions as Lewis acid catalysts
conditions.other: requires metal-ion catalysis for prebiotic transamination; Fe2+ abundant on early Earth; may occur at mineral-water interfaces

reactions.mechanism: Alanine forms a Schiff base with α-ketoisovalerate (or with a metal-bound pyridoxal analog). The amino group is transferred from alanine to the keto acid via a tautomeric shift through the ketimine-aldimine equilibrium, yielding valine + pyruvate. Fe2+ or Cu2+ coordinates both substrates and lowers the activation barrier for the 1,3-prototropic shift.
reactions.reasoning: Transamination is the biological route to valine (via branched-chain aminotransferase). Prebiotic transamination has been demonstrated with metal-ion catalysts (Fe2+, Cu2+) substituting for pyridoxal phosphate. Miller & Schlesinger (1993) noted that metal salts catalyze α-ketoisovalerate reactions. This pathway regenerates pyruvate, creating a proto-metabolic cycle. While less efficient than enzymatic transamination, it provides an alternative route to valine when α-ketoisovalerate and alanine co-occur.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate on magnetite

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent, multiple CO2 reduction steps on mineral surface

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction on iron oxide/sulfide surfaces: CO2 → formate → formaldehyde → acetaldehyde (via surface-bound intermediates) → pyruvate via further carboxylation and oxidation. The mineral surface provides a template for stepwise carbon fixation, analogous to the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate from CO2 + H2 on iron mineral surfaces has been demonstrated experimentally. Magnetite and greigite catalyze these multi-step reductions at alkaline vent conditions. Pyruvate is a central metabolic hub in both biology and prebiotic chemistry. It serves as both a precursor to alanine (via rxn_018) and a transamination product (rxn_019).

reactions.name: Miller-Urey spark discharge synthesis of valine

conditions.catalyst: electric discharge (lightning analog); mineral dust (TiO2) may serve as nucleation surface
conditions.other: reducing atmosphere: CH4 + NH3 + H2O + H2; spark produces HCN, aldehydes, which undergo Strecker-type chemistry upon cooling and dissolution in water

reactions.mechanism: Electric discharge through a reducing gas mixture (CH4, NH3, H2O, H2) fragments molecules into reactive radicals (CH, CH2, NH, CN). Upon cooling, these recombine to form HCN, aldehydes (including trace isobutyraldehyde), and NH3. In the aqueous phase, Strecker-type chemistry converts these to aminonitriles, which hydrolyze to amino acids. Valine is a minor product alongside dominant glycine and alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Johnson et al. (2009) reanalyzed Miller's original 1953 spark-discharge samples and detected valine and isovaline among ~40 amino acids. While valine is only a trace product (glycine/alanine dominate), its presence confirms that branched amino acids can arise from simple reducing gas mixtures under spark-discharge conditions. This represents an atmospheric/lightning route to valine.

reactions.name: Atmospheric HCN synthesis from methane and nitrogen

conditions.other: atmospheric chemistry; CH4 + N sources (NH3, N2) → HCN via radical reactions; HCN dissolves in rain and accumulates in surface pools

reactions.mechanism: UV photolysis or lightning discharge fragments CH4 into CH and CH2 radicals, and NH3 into NH radicals. CH + NH recombine to form HCN. Alternatively, N2 + CH4 under strong UV or discharge yields HCN directly. HCN accumulates in the atmosphere and dissolves in rainwater, concentrating in surface pools and lakes.
reactions.reasoning: Atmospheric HCN production is a well-established source of prebiotic cyanide. HCN is the critical C1/N building block for both Strecker synthesis and cyanosulfidic chemistry. Miller-Urey experiments demonstrate HCN as a primary product of spark discharge (Johnson et al., 2009). HCN dissolved in surface water feeds into all downstream cyanide-dependent pathways.

pathways.name: Full Patel cyanosulfidic pathway (formose to valine)
pathways.description: Complete cyanosulfidic route starting from formaldehyde through formose chemistry to glyceraldehyde, then photoreduction to acetone, followed by Patel et al.'s (2015) four-step homologation to valinonitrile, ending with hydrolysis to valine. This is the most experimentally validated prebiotic pathway to valine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO2 to cyanosulfidic valine (cross-environment)
pathways.description: Starting from hydrothermal CO2 fixation to formate, then surface photoreduction to formaldehyde, entering the formose/cyanosulfidic cascade to valine. Demonstrates Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical environmental flow.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic isobutyraldehyde to classical Strecker
pathways.description: Cyanosulfidic pathway generates cyanohydrin 26, which undergoes reversible dissociation to release free isobutyraldehyde. This aldehyde then enters classical Strecker synthesis (isobutyraldehyde + NH3 + HCN → valinonitrile → valine). Connects Patel photochemistry with Miller-Schlesinger Strecker route.

pathways.name: DAP-mediated Strecker at neutral pH
pathways.description: Isobutyraldehyde (from cyanosulfidic cyanohydrin dissociation) undergoes Strecker synthesis at neutral pH mediated by diamidophosphate (DAP), as demonstrated by Powner et al. (2019). This route overcomes the pH constraint of classical Strecker (which needs pH > 9 for free NH3).

pathways.name: Kaur reductive amination from cyanosulfidic α-ketoisovalerate
pathways.description: Isobutyraldehyde from the cyanosulfidic pathway is converted to α-hydroxyisovaleric acid (via cyanohydrin hydrolysis), then oxidized to α-ketoisovalerate, and finally reduced to valine by H2 on Ni minerals. This connects Patel's surface chemistry to Kaur et al.'s hydrothermal reductive amination.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination (shortest from α-ketoisovalerate)
pathways.description: Direct reductive amination of α-ketoisovalerate with H2 and NH3 on Ni catalyst, as demonstrated by Kaur et al. (2024). This is the most direct route when α-ketoisovalerate is available, requiring only one reaction step.

pathways.name: Transamination pathway via alanine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis, followed by reductive amination to alanine (on Ni), then metal-catalyzed transamination of α-ketoisovalerate with alanine to yield valine + pyruvate. This creates a proto-metabolic autocatalytic cycle where pyruvate is regenerated.

pathways.name: Full cross-environment transamination (Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical)
pathways.description: CO2 is fixed to pyruvate hydrothermally; pyruvate is reductively aminated to alanine. In parallel, the cyanosulfidic surface pathway generates isobutyraldehyde, which is converted to α-ketoisovalerate via cyanohydrin hydrolysis and oxidation. Alanine and α-ketoisovalerate then undergo transamination to valine in the biochemical regime.

pathways.name: Miller-Urey spark discharge to valine
pathways.description: Classical Miller-Urey atmospheric chemistry: electric discharge through a reducing gas mixture (CH4, NH3, H2O, H2) produces valine as a minor product among dozens of amino acids. As confirmed by Johnson et al. (2009) reanalysis of Miller's original 1953 samples.

pathways.name: Atmospheric HCN to cyanosulfidic valine
pathways.description: HCN is generated atmospherically from CH4 and N-sources, dissolves in surface waters, and drives the cyanosulfidic pathway: HCN provides both the cyanohydrin carbon and the aminonitrile nitrogen. This pathway traces HCN from its atmospheric source through all cyanosulfidic intermediates to valine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent recapitulation of Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic photochemistry. Each step is literature-validated with ~42% yield for the 26→27 conversion. The formose initiation (rxn_003) could benefit from explicit mineral catalyst specification (e.g., borate, Ca(OH)2). The photoreductive homologation step (rxn_009) is the most complex and potentially lowest-yield step. The transition from Surface to Biochemical for the final hydrolysis is justified. Missing explicit mention of Cu(I)/Cu(II) photocatalyst in surface reactions reduces environmental scoring slightly.
suggestions: Specify Ca(OH)2 or borate mineral for formose initiation in rxn_003
Explicitly include Cu(I)/Cu(II) or CuS mineral photocatalyst for rxn_006, rxn_008, rxn_009
Consider adding phosphate buffer specification for cyanosulfidic steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent cross-environment pathway demonstrating Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical flow. The CO2 hydrogenation on greigite (rxn_001) is well-supported. However, rxn_002 (formate photoreduction to formaldehyde) is the weakest link — this step is less experimentally validated than other reactions and lacks mineral catalyst specification. The UV photoreduction of formate to formaldehyde, while thermodynamically feasible, has limited prebiotic literature support. The rest of the pathway inherits the strengths of P1.
suggestions: Replace rxn_002 with a better-documented route to formaldehyde, e.g., Cannizzaro reaction or direct hydrothermal reduction
Specify mineral photocatalyst (e.g., TiO2 or ZnS) for formate photoreduction
Consider atmospheric formaldehyde production as alternative entry point

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Clever convergent route using cyanosulfidic chemistry to generate isobutyraldehyde (via cyanohydrin 26 dissociation), then classical Strecker. The cyanohydrin dissociation (rxn_011) is thermodynamically reasonable and provides a prebiotic source of isobutyraldehyde — directly addressing the open challenge noted in literature. The Strecker step (rxn_012) is highly literature-validated. However, the pathway stays entirely in the Surface environment, missing the cross-environment progression ideal.
suggestions: Quantify the equilibrium of cyanohydrin dissociation — at what pH/conditions does this favor isobutyraldehyde release?
Add Zn2+ or mineral catalyst for the Strecker imine formation step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Variant of P3 using DAP-mediated Strecker (Powner et al. 2019). The use of DAP solves the pH constraint problem noted in literature (NH3 requires basic pH, but HCN is volatile at high pH). The 73-quantitative% yields reported for phosphoroaminonitriles support this route. DAP availability requires additional justification (e.g., from schreibersite hydrolysis). Overall a strong, literature-backed pathway.
suggestions: Add a reaction showing DAP generation from schreibersite or wet-dry cycling
Note that DAP-mediated route works at neutral pH, which is a significant advantage

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Novel integration of cyanosulfidic precursor generation with Kaur et al. reductive amination. The route through cyanohydrin hydrolysis to α-hydroxyisovaleric acid (rxn_015) and then oxidation to α-ketoisovalerate (rxn_016) is chemically plausible but rxn_016 is the weakest link — the oxidation step lacks specification of the oxidant (UV/O2/mineral oxidant?). The final Ni-catalyzed reductive amination (rxn_017) is strongly supported by Kaur et al. 2024. Good Surface→Hydrothermal environmental transition.
suggestions: Specify the oxidant for rxn_016: UV-driven oxidation with mineral photocatalyst, or Fe(III) mineral oxidant
Consider whether α-ketoisovalerate could form directly without the hydroxy acid intermediate
Note that Kaur et al. used 5 bar H2, 150 mM NH4HCO3, Ni/SiO2-Al2O3 — specify these conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Single-step pathway that only shows the final Ni-catalyzed reductive amination. While the reaction itself is strongly supported by Kaur et al. 2024, this pathway lacks precursor justification — α-ketoisovalerate is assumed available without showing its synthesis. As a standalone pathway it is incomplete, though it serves as a useful terminal node in the broader network.
suggestions: This should not be considered a standalone pathway — it is a terminal reaction segment
Extend to include α-ketoisovalerate supply route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Transamination pathway is conceptually interesting — mimicking biological amino acid metabolism. However, rxn_020 (CO2 + H2 → pyruvate on magnetite in one step) is a significant stretch; while Varma et al. and others have shown CO2 reduction to simple organics, direct pyruvate synthesis is low-yield and poorly documented as a single step. The transamination (rxn_019) is well-known biologically but prebiotically requires metal catalysts (Fe, Cu, pyridoxal-like cofactors) not specified. The overall pathway has several weak links.
suggestions: Break rxn_020 into multiple steps (CO2 → CO → acetyl-thioester → pyruvate)
Specify metal catalyst for transamination (e.g., Fe2+, Cu2+, or pyridoxamine)
Add oxidant specification for rxn_016

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Admirable cross-environment pathway showing full Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical progression. The environmental transitions are well-justified (hydrothermal CO2 fixation feeds surface photochemistry, which feeds biochemical transamination). However, it inherits weaknesses from rxn_002, rxn_016, and rxn_019. The transamination step in the Biochemical environment needs better catalyst justification for prebiotic plausibility.
suggestions: Strengthen rxn_002 with mineral photocatalyst
Specify oxidant and conditions for rxn_016
Add metal catalyst for transamination step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: While Miller-Urey experiments have detected valine (Johnson et al. 2009), this is an over-simplified single-reaction representation of a complex multi-step process. Valine was only a trace product in Miller-Urey experiments (glycine/alanine dominated). The reaction lumps dozens of intermediate steps into one node, providing no mechanistic insight. Not useful as a standalone pathway, though it provides important historical context for the network.
suggestions: Decompose into intermediate steps: spark → HCN + aldehydes → Strecker intermediates → amino acids
Note that valine yield was trace-level in original experiments
Better used as supplementary context rather than a primary pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good pathway integrating atmospheric HCN synthesis with cyanosulfidic photochemistry. The atmospheric HCN formation (rxn_022) is well-documented (requires UV or spark energy). Feeds into the Patel et al. pathway from the acetone stage. Assumes acetone is already available, which requires the formose/DHA branch — this pathway may be missing some earlier steps for completeness. Overall sound chemistry with good literature support.
suggestions: Clarify how acetone is supplied — P10 seems to start at rxn_007 which requires acetone from rxn_006
Add explicit energy source (UV/lightning) for rxn_022

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an exceptionally well-designed prebiotic synthesis network for valine that demonstrates strong literature alignment, excellent network topology, and sophisticated integration of multiple validated routes. The network's greatest strengths are: (1) faithful recapitulation of Patel et al.'s cyanosulfidic photochemistry with proper intermediate numbering (compounds 23-27), (2) integration of four distinct literature-validated routes (Patel cyanosulfidic, classical Strecker, Kaur reductive amination, Miller-Urey), (3) elegant solution to the key open challenge of branched C4 skeleton supply via cyanosulfidic isobutyraldehyde generation, (4) well-chosen hub molecules creating genuine network density with 5 convergence points, and (5) proper cross-environment flow. Minor weaknesses include: rxn_002 (formate photoreduction) lacking mineral specification and limited literature support, rxn_016 (hydroxy acid oxidation) missing oxidant specification, rxn_020 (CO2 to pyruvate) being over-simplified, and the Miller-Urey reaction (rxn_021) being an opaque single-step black box. Several Surface reactions should explicitly specify mineral catalysts (Cu(I)/Cu(II) for photochemistry, Ca(OH)2/borate for formose). The transamination pathway (rxn_019) needs prebiotic catalyst justification. Despite these issues, the top pathways (P1, P2, P3, P4, P5) are all chemically sound and literature-supported, making this a high-quality network. The strategy of tracing carbon from CO2/formaldehyde through formose chemistry to glyceraldehyde, then branching through cyanosulfidic and cyanohydrin pathways, is both creative and scientifically rigorous.
